Why Writing Posts Can Earn Money — Google AdSense and Liker, Explained
Did you know you can make money writing blog posts? And that Google AdSense isn't the only way? This post is the entry-level take on how blog revenue actually works.
A few things to know first

Before you read on — make sure you have your own blog. Otherwise reading this is wasted effort, because you'd just be doing the work on a platform that doesn't pay you. Or worse — making someone else money.
If you're a developer and need a starting point, I'd recommend
my Hexo blog setup post (article in Mandarin)
don't put your posts on Facebook or Instagram
If you've been online for any amount of time, you've seen plenty of annoying ads. My blog has more than a few too:
So why does writing pay? Most of us write to share something we know or to capture something we've learned. For me, it's tech notes and life stuff.
And it just so happens that your posts are useful to some other person — so they hit Google or Yahoo, find your article, and land on your site.
Example — I wrote a post titled something like "What's ESM (ES6 Modules / JavaScript Modules)?" When someone searches "

. (Google's algorithm shifts constantly, so today's #1 won't be #1 forever.) Most readers click within the top 5 results — nobody pages through results from the back, just like you don't grocery-shop starting from the freezer aisle. So SEO keyword tuning matters.
When that reader lands on your site and you've got Google AdSense set up, you earn. AdSense pays you in two ways:
— if a reader's eye catches an ad while browsing your site and they click, you get paid for that click.

Click revenue is usually higher per event than impression revenue, because someone who actively clicks usually has real interest. As a real example — I've been looking at online courses lately, so AdSense keeps showing me course ads.
— as long as an ad shows up on your screen (computer, tablet, phone), you earn something. Like this very example image:
If you can see the ad, you've generated impression revenue. Note —
neither type counts your own views
. And don't click your own ads.
But if a reader has AdBlock on, nothing pays. So what to do? A gentle nudge in the page can ask readers to whitelist your site to support you:

Google AdSense income is genuinely volatile — unless you're consistently posting good content, expect modest numbers. For a sense of scale: $5 per 1,000 impressions is already a decent figure for AdSense.
So AdSense is mostly "set it up and let it work" passive income. How to convince readers to disable AdBlock is its own separate problem.
If you want more detail on growing AdSense revenue, my earlier post
How to Increase Your Google AdSense Revenue (Placement Tips)
— "turn likes into rewards". When a reader likes your post, you earn.

What's Liker? In short, it's a like-button mechanism built on blockchain. If you don't know what that means — you've probably heard of Bitcoin? Same family. When a reader finds your post useful and clicks Like, those likes convert into LikerCoin that go to your account. Plain English — you get money.

Important — Liker isn't a fake virtual currency. LikerCoin is a real crypto you can exchange for fiat through an exchange, same as Bitcoin. If you held 1,000 LikerCoin, here's roughly what that'd be worth according to CoinGecko:
So getting readers to actually like your posts matters. A little CTA in the article footer reminding them goes a long way:
Each reader can give up to 5 likes per article, and 5 likes is about 1.x LikerCoin:
So more likes per article is better — the allocation is bigger. That's Liker's design — readers can throw you a tip via likes, you have an incentive to keep creating.
. Readers who really value your content can become Civic Likers and tip you ~$5/month via Liker.
It's a higher bar — but it happens. As your Civic Liker count grows, so does your LikerCoin allocation. For 30 Civic Likers, the official estimate is something like this per month:
So writing posts isn't just about you — your readers benefit, and they can give back through AdSense and Liker, which makes it easier to keep creating.
After reading this, are you considering setting up your own platform to write?
If you want to support me, you can become a Civic Liker through
Quick checklist of what you actually need to make passive income from blogging:
A platform where you can put Google AdSense and Liker
Keep up with search engine algorithm changes
Have readers actually engage with likes / tips
Running a blog can be simple — or really hard. With effort, you can make a small income from writing.
Hope this post nudges you to start (or keep) creating, rather than scaring you off.
